sections.heading: Surgery
sections.content:
Surgery plays a limited but critical role in the management of anal carcinoma. The primary treatment for most anal canal cancers is organ-preserving concurrent chemoRT; surgery is reserved for (1) very early perianal lesions amenable to local excision, (2) biopsy-proven recurrent or persistent disease after primary chemoRT (APR), and (3) inguinal node recurrence (groin dissection) [MS-15, MS-19]. Radical surgery (APR) is the primary treatment for local recurrence/persistence following failed chemoRT [ANAL-A].

principles: [
"Local excision is used for SISCCA and select early perianal cancers with histologically negative margins [ANAL-A]",
"For SISCCA, local excision alone with a structured surveillance plan may represent adequate treatment in carefully selected patients followed by an experienced provider and/or team [ANAL-A, MS-16]",
"APR for anal cancer may require wider lateral perianal margins than are required for rectal cancer [ANAL-A, MS-19]",
"General principles for APR are similar to those for distal rectal cancer and include the incorporation of total mesorectal excision (TME) [ANAL-A, MS-19]",
"Reconstructive tissue flaps (vertical rectus or local myocutaneous flaps) should be considered due to poor perineal wound healing following prior RT [ANAL-A, MS-19; 227, 228]",
"For patients who have already received groin radiation, consider an inguinal node dissection [ANAL-A]",
"Groin dissection can be done with or without APR depending on whether disease is isolated to the groin or is in conjunction with recurrence/persistence at the primary site [ANAL-A, MS-19]"
]

resectability_criteria: APR is indicated for biopsy-proven locally recurrent or persistent disease following primary chemoRT [ANAL-3, ANAL-4]. In an attempt to avoid surgery, the use of immunotherapy (nivolumab, pembrolizumab, retifanlimab-dlwr, cemiplimab-rwlc, dostarlimab-gxly, tislelizumab-jsgr, toripalimab-tpzi, or penpulimab-kcqx) (category 2B) may be considered prior to APR; institutional experience has demonstrated that some patients receive a good response and can avoid surgery [ANAL-3 footnote p, MS-19]. Factors associated with worse prognosis following APR include an initial presentation of node-positive disease and RT doses <55 Gy used in the treatment of primary disease [217].

pre_operative_optimisation: Reconstructive tissue flaps for the perineum, such as the vertical rectus or local myocutaneous flaps, should be considered for patients with anal cancer undergoing an APR due to the necessary exposure of the perineum to radiation and the resulting poor perine

cards.name: Local Excision (for SISCCA)
cards.detail:
approach: Transanal or perianal excision
indication: Superficially invasive squamous cell carcinoma (SISCCA): anal cancer that has been completely excised, with ≤3-mm basement membrane invasion and a maximal horizontal spread of ≤7 mm (T1,NX). Generally found incidentally in the setting of biopsy or excision of a benign lesion such as a condyloma, hemorrhoid, or anal skin tag [ANAL-A, 194]
key_complications: Local recurrence risk; HSIL detected in 74% of patients following local excision in observational studies [195]
operative_mortality: Not reported specifically for this procedure

cards.name: Local Excision (for Perianal Cancer)
cards.detail:
approach: Surgical excision with 1-cm margins
indication: T1N0, well or moderately differentiated or select T2N0 squamous cell carcinoma of the perianal (anal margin) region. Where the tumor forms a discrete lesion arising from the perianal skin clearly separate from the anal canal, where negative margin excision can be accomplished without compromise of adjacent sphincter muscles, and where there is no evidence of regional nodal involvement [ANAL-A, MS-16]
key_complications: Positive margins (if <1 cm), local recurrence
operative_mortality: Not reported specifically for this procedure

cards.name: Abdominoperineal Resection (APR)
cards.detail:
approach: Open or minimally invasive surgical resection with colostomy, incorporating TME
indication: Biopsy-proven locally recurrent or persistent disease following primary chemoRT [ANAL-3, ANAL-4, MS-19]
key_complications: Perineal wound healing complications (especially after prior RT; rectus abdominis myocutaneous flap reconstruction may decrease complications [227, 228]), sexual dysfunction, urinary dysfunction [MS-19, MS-23]
operative_mortality: 5-year survival rates of 39% to 66% have been observed in studies involving a minimum of 25 patients; complication rates reported to be high [MS-19; 216, 217, 221-225]

cards.name: Inguinal Node Dissection
cards.detail:
approach: Open surgical dissection of inguinal lymph nodes
indication: Inguinal node recurrence; patients who require an APR but have already received groin radiation [ANAL-A, ANAL-3, MS-19]
key_complications: Lymphedema, wound complications, infection
operative_mortality: Not reported specifically for this procedure
